data.title: The Gabelli Convertible And Income Securities Fund Inc. Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Objective

The Gabelli Convertible and Income Securities Fund (GCV) is a diversified, closed-end management investment company whose primary investment objective is to seek a high level of total return through a combination of current income and capital appreciation.

Fund Performance

Gabelli Convertible and Income Securities Fund significantly outperformed the ICE BAML All Convertibles Index for the quarter with returns of 15.0% relative to the index at 8.6%.

Convertibles were a top performing asset class in the quarter, driven by strong underlying equity performance. Markets have looked past geopolitical risk, tariffs, and some mixed economic data to march higher, benefiting the convertible market broadly. At the end of the quarter, we saw our first interest rate cut of the year, and we expect more to follow. This should be a tailwind for our market in the form of increasing bond floors and earnings multiple expansions. We believe convertibles offer a great opportunity to maintain risk adjusted equity exposure in this environment.

*Percentage of portfolio as of September 30, 2025

The Fund's top performing convertibles this quarter included Bloom Energy 3% of '28, MP Materials 3% of '30, and Lumentum 1.5% of '29. Our top detractors were Progress Software 3.5% of '30 and Vertex 0.75% of '29.

Convertible issuance continued at a torrid pace in the quarter, increasing the total market to $388 billion while broadening the pool of convertibles available to us. This has been beneficial to performance in the near term. Pricing has been driven by arbitrage demand, leading to lower coupons and higher premiums, which could prove to be a drag on the market over time. We are participating in these issues where appropriate, but continue to build our portfolio around convertibles that we believe have asymmetrical upside potential.

At current levels, the convertible market offers a YTM of 2.7% and a 31% premium to conversion value. Our portfolio offers a 2.7% YTM at a 30% conversion premium. Sensitivity to moves in underlying equities has increased, with the market delta now at 62. GCV’s convertible portfolio is in line with the market with a delta of 62. At quarter end our portfolio was 19% equity sensitive, 65% total return, and 16% fixed income equivalent. This compares to the broad market at 44% equity, 36% total return, and 20% fixed income equivalent.

data.title: French Business Sentiment Lifted By Services
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

The French business climate indicator rose to 98 from 97 in October, according to Insee. The improvement is largely due to services, where confidence gained 3 points on more favourable expectations for demand and activity. The PMI survey, also released this morning, confirms the trend: the services index hit its highest level in 15 months, signalling a cyclical rebound.

By contrast, industry is flashing warning signs. The manufacturing PMI fell to its lowest in nine months, and the business climate reflects weaker order books and production prospects. Transport equipment firms are particularly downbeat after a strong growth phase. Momentum in this sector appears to be fading, pointing to a weaker industrial contribution from Q4 onwards and into 2026. While some cyclical improvement in certain industrial sub-sectors is likely, the slowdown in transport will weigh on overall industrial growth. Confidence in retail also slipped slightly but remains well above September levels.

Limited growth expected in 2026

Overall, the French economy looks uneven: industry, notably aerospace, is losing steam, while services benefit from renewed confidence. This uptick, helped by political calm despite the absence of a 2026 budget, could support consumption in the fourth quarter. However, the recovery rests on fragile foundations and may fade quickly.

Fiscal uncertainty will remain a major drag. We expect no budget agreement before year-end, meaning the 2025 budget will roll over into 2026. Fiscal tightening will then be introduced gradually, but it is unlikely the deficit will fall to 4.6% of GDP as promised by the government (5% seems more realistic, after 5.4% in 2025). A more restrictive fiscal stance will cap French growth, which should stay below the eurozone average: we forecast GDP growth of 0.9% in 2026 after 0.8% in 2025, compared with 1.1% for the eurozone (after 1.4% in 2025).

In short, while the rebound in services offers temporary relief, tighter fiscal policy signals a moderate trajectory for the French economy.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.description:
Note: The image above shows copper-cobalt ore.

Welcome to the November 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices slightly higher.

Cobalt price news

As of November 19, the cobalt spot price was at US$22.03/lb, slightly higher from US$21.37/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$47,640/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 126 tonnes, same as 126 from last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long term price chart - USD 22.03/lb (source)

Cobalt demand v supply forecasts

The new DRC export quota system could potentially flip the ex-DRC market balance from a cobalt surplus to a deficit in 2025 (RHS chart) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

Cobalt market news

On November 3 Argus Media reported:

Indonesian cobalt supply rises despite DRC export curbs..."Global actual cobalt feedstock supply will drop to 198,000t of metal equivalent in 2025, down by 38pc from 2024, due to the DRC's cobalt export restrictions, although feedstock production reached 288,000t of metal equivalent during the period," Xu told delegates at the 2025 Nickel and Cobalt Industry Conference in Lanzhou city in northwest China's Gansu province. Indonesian cobalt supply increased to 44,000t of metal equivalent this year from 34,000t in 2024 and is projected to continue rising to 90,000t by 2030, indicating reduced reliance on DRC-sourced cobalt feedstock.

On November 14 Reuters reported:

Chinese battery material makers push for higher prices as cobalt rally hits supply chain. Suppliers to China's electric-vehicle battery makers are pushing for higher prices for key cobalt-based materials, aided by a sharp rebound in cobalt and a tightening market for raw materials in batteries...have sought higher offers for long-term contracts in recent weeks, according to three traders and producers...Cobalt traded near $24 a pound on Thursday, up from around $10 per pound before Congo's export ban was rolled out in late February, helping to end a three-year slump for the battery metal..."The rebalancing of supply and demand has slightly increased battery and supply chain pricing from trough levels," said Yuqian Ding, head of China autos research at HSBC, in a November 11 note.

Cobalt company news

CMOC Group Limited [HKSE:3993] [SHE:603993] (OTCPK:CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

On October 28 Market Screener announced: "Jefferies adjusts CMOC Group's price target to HK$19.50 from HK$12.60, keeps at buy."

On October 29 Bankable Africa reported:

CMOC’s nine-month cobalt output doubles its 2025-2026 export quotas...Most of this year’s production, however, cannot be exported... For 2026, the company will be allowed to export 31,200 tons, based on December 2025’s authorized volume being maintained each month, unless regulations are breached. Over the two-year period, CMOC’s cumulative exports will be capped at 37,700 tons, less than half its output for the first nine months of 2025. Executives have described the situation as “barely tolerable”...The quota regime is expected to remain in place until at least 2027...Kinshasa hopes the new rules will lift cobalt prices and encourage miners to invest in local refining and value-added production. Unofficial reports suggest the government is targeting a floor price of $60,000 per tonne.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

On October 29 Glencore announced: "Third Quarter 2025 Production Report... Own sourced cobalt production of 28,500 tonnes was 2,000 tonnes (8%) higher than the comparable 2024 period, reflecting higher cobalt grades and volumes at Mutanda."

Note: FY 2025 production guidance is 41-43kt and Glencore state: "A quota system currently applies to DRC cobalt exports. Production volumes above the allocated quotas continue to be stored in-country."

Chilean Cobalt Corp. enters Offtake Arrangement with Glencore on La Cobaltera and El Cofre Projects...The Company will provide Glencore with an irrevocable and exclusive right to purchase up to 100% of cobalt and/or copper containing products...Both parties anticipate that of any Materials sold to Glencore in the future, the majority of the cobalt and copper intermediate product stream will be shipped into the United States ("US") for processing and refining...In addition, Glencore is expected to provide strategic support for development of Chilean Cobalt's La Cobaltera Phase 1 cobalt-copper project, and to collaborate on new initiatives aimed at developing US-based processing and refining capabilities for metal products and/or battery materials.

Conclusion

November saw cobalt spot prices slightly higher over the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Indonesian cobalt supply rises despite DRC export curbs. "Global actual cobalt feedstock supply will drop to 198,000t of metal equivalent in 2025, down by 38pc from 2024, due to the DRC's cobalt export restrictions, although feedstock production reached 288,000t of metal equivalent during the period.
  • Suppliers to China's electric-vehicle battery makers are pushing for higher prices for key cobalt-based materials, aided by a sharp rebound in cobalt and a tightening market for raw materials in batteries.
  • CMOC Group's 2026 DRC export quota is 31,200t. Unofficial reports suggest the DRC government is targeting a floor price of $60,000/t, cobalt currently trades at around $45,000/t on the LME (as of late Oct. 2025).
  • Glencore's 2026 DRC export quota is 18,800t.
  • Huayou Cobalt DRC subsidiary Congo Dongfang International Mining -Congo suspends activities at Chinese mine after spill.
  • GEM Co issues its first tech innovation-themed medium-term note.
  • Sumitomo Metal Mining's Fiscal H1 profit jumps 16%.
  • Sherritt International Q3 adjusted net loss from continuing operations was $15.5 million...
  • Nickel 28 - Ramu Q3 Cobalt sales of 948t of contained cobalt, compared to 798t in the same period last year.
  • Electra reactivates construction at Cobalt Sulfate Refinery in North America, targeted commissioning in 2027.
  • Sunrise Energy Metals - Successful A$46m placement to fund pre-construction activities at Syerston Scandium Project.
  • Cobalt Blue - Advancing Black Mass recycling at the Broken Hill Technology Centre.
  • Havilah Resources and Sandfire Resources agree to a A$210M (scrip & cash) binding deal to advance the Kalkaroo Copper-Gold Project and regional exploration.
  • Alliance Nickel-Stellantis Offtake Agreement to be renegotiated.
  • GEMC announces termination of Luna Energy transaction.
  • The Metals Corp. announces a net loss of $20.5 million for the quarter ended September 30, 2024.
  • Chilean Cobalt Corp. enters Offtake Arrangement with Glencore on La Cobaltera and El Cofre Projects.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: Provident Financial Services: I'm Not Ready To Stop Banking On Upside
data.description:
To be honest with you, I am a little disappointed with how shares of Provident Financial Services, Inc. (PFS) have performed as of late. Back in August of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was after the stock had performed nicely compared to the broader market and was driven in part by the completion of its merger with Lakeland Bancorp. Even though revenue and profits have risen nicely, and even though the size of the firm's balance sheet has gotten larger, shares have underperformed the market, inching up by only 0.9% while the S&P 500 is up 6.2%. I guess the good news is that, compared to when I initially rated the firm a ‘buy’ back in February of last year, the stock has performed better, rising by 32.5%. But even so, that does fall short of the 37.2% eyes that the S&P 500 saw.

Looking at the picture today, I see a business that's continuing to achieve strong year-over-year growth. The aforementioned merger certainly helped to boost overall assets. And on top of this, asset quality remains high while shares are cheap relative to earnings and book value. When you add all of these things together, it's difficult not to be bullish about this name. So although the market has disagreed with my assessment as of late, I believe that maintaining the business as a ‘buy’ candidate is the right choice.

Still banking on Provident Financial Services

The newest data that we have regarding Provident Financial Services covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. This is only one additional operating quarter compared to when I last analyzed the bank. During the quarter, deposits for the institution came in at $19.10 billion. That was up nicely from the $18.62 billion the company had on its books at the end of last year. This is great to see in and of itself. However, all of this deposit growth and more came from high-cost brokered deposits that the institution got from other financial organizations. That metric grew from $255 million at the end of last year to $805.9 million in the third quarter of this year. This helped to offset weakness in savings accounts and, to a lesser extent, in non-interest-bearing deposit accounts. But in general, I'm not a fan of seeing such a big move higher in brokered deposits.

One other thing that's worth mentioning when it comes to deposits would be exposure to uninsured deposits. I typically like to see this metric come in at 30% of total deposits and sure enough, when we strip out collateralized uninsured deposits, we get exposure amounting to 25.2%. This means to me that the institution has a lower risk profile than it otherwise would if uninsured deposits were higher. After all, the higher that uninsured deposits are, the higher the risk is during a crisis that a bank run will occur.

The value of loans in the most recent quarter came in at $19.10 billion. That represented a nice move higher over the $18.63 billion the company ended last year with. The greatest exposure that Provident Financial Services has when it comes to loans would be the $7.32 billion of commercial loans on its books. That accounts for approximately 37.9% of its entire loan portfolio. Outside of this, commercial loans amounted to $4.84 billion, while multifamily loans came in at $3.53 billion. One thing I will say is that whenever I see a lot of exposure to commercial loans, specifically commercial real estate like the $10.98 billion worth of the company has on its books, I am always worried about exposure to office assets because of high and rising vacancy rates. But the good news is that only 4.1% of its entire loan portfolio is dedicated to these types of assets. That is quite low and puts the company near the lowest of the banks that I look at.

We should also pay attention to other aspects of the balance sheet. During the quarter, the company had securities amounting to $3.57 billion. That was up from the $3.12 billion reported a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, jumped from $205.9 million to $301.6 million. And finally, debt on its books grew, but only a bit, climbing from $2.42 billion to $2.61 billion.

When it comes to the income statement, I am happy to say that we have seen some nice growth year over year. The changes in the size of the company's balance sheet, combined with a rise in its net interest margin from 3.31% to 3.43%, took its net interest income up from $174.4 million in the third quarter of last year to $187.3 million at the same time this year. One thing that also helped here was a decline in its provision for credit losses from $9.3 million down to only $7 million. And for the first nine months of this year, these same improvements pushed net interest income up to $558.4 million compared to the $340.2 million reported last year.

Non-interest income for the institution has also been growing. For the most recent quarter, this amounted to $27.4 million, which represented a small increase over the $26.9 million reported a year earlier. Surprisingly, wealth management income actually contracted from $7.6 million to $7.3 million. But even with that, things would have been better had it not been for a decline in bank-owned life insurance revenue from $4.3 million to $2.7 million. The improvements to offset this involved fees revenue, insurance agency income, and other miscellaneous income, all rising year over year. A similar trend can be seen when looking at the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year, with an increase from $69.9 million to $81.5 million.

That ultimately pushed net profits up significantly from $67 million to $207.7 million. And for the most recent quarter on its own, the $71.7 million generated by the company meaningfully outperformed the $46.4 million reported last year. It is worth noting that some, in fact a lot of the improvement in profitability here came from merger-related expenses last year that were not repeated this year. That included $15.6 million in the third quarter, with that number growing to $36.7 million for the first nine months of last year compared to this year.

If we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would get a price-to-earnings multiple for the institution of 8.6. As the chart above illustrates, this puts the company cheaper than any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. It's also comfortably below the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. The good news is that, on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis, the stock is cheap compared to those of banks. In the chart below, you can see that only one of the five firms is cheaper than it is using these metrics.

On top of boasting low trading multiples and improving fundamentals, another thing that Provident Financial Services has going for it is the fact that asset quality appears robust. In the first chart below, you can see that the firm has a return on assets reading of 1.16%. That's higher than the 1% minimum I like to see, and it puts the company around the middle of the group when talking about the aforementioned firms. Its ranking is similar when using its return on equity, which is shown in the next chart below. The 10.39% that we get here is above the 10% minimum I prefer. And three of the five institutions are lower than our candidate on this basis.

Takeaway

Right now, I think that things are going pretty well for Provident Financial Services. The company is doing a solid job. Revenue, profits, and balance sheet items are all mostly getting better. Net interest margin is expanding, and asset quality is really good. When you add all of these things together, it's difficult not to rate the enterprise a ‘buy’ at this time.

data.title: Palantir: AI Exuberance Likely To Resume Before Year-End
data.description:
In my last coverage on Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) (PLTR:CA), I upgraded my rating on the AI juggernaut as I identified a potential tailwind in the international segment after a UK strategic partnership was announced. I said,

I believe Palantir's UK strategic partnership should catalyze international government growth from future NATO and European MoD wins.

Well, Q3 results showed (once again) why this segment needs to return to growth. The US commercial segment grew its top line by 121% yoy in the past quarter. In comparison, Q3 international commercial revenue grew by a mere 10% yoy. I reiterate once more that the company needs to show strength in the international commercial segment. On the other hand, the international government segment reported a 66% yoy increase. As I initially anticipated in my last coverage, this is mainly driven by the continued government work in the U.K.

That said, I argue that an immediate improvement in the international commercial segment is not a necessary condition for the stock to return to all-time highs. The near-term focus is mainly centered in the US, where both the US commercial and government sectors are outperforming.

From a fundamentals perspective, I don't see any material signs to suggest a deterioration in this quarter or early 2026. The stock's underperformance is mainly driven by a deterioration in sentiment after Burry's short bet (in the form of put options). Overall, I believe the timing of his short is way too early, as, again, I see no signs of deterioration in the AI narrative. If I start to see hyperscalers cutting down CapEx guidance, OpenAI failing to pay the contracted deals, and Palantir's US commercial segment deteriorating, then I may reassess my strong buy rating. Until then, I remain hyper bullish on AI stocks.

Q3 2025 Overview

The company surprised on both the top line and bottom line, in addition to raising FY 2025 guidance. Let's start with the top line first.

Revenue came in at $1.181 billion, up 63% yoy and 18% sequentially. Most of the sales originated in the US, where revenue increased by 77% yoy and 20% qoq. Q4 2025 revenue was guided in the range of $1.327 to $1.331 billion, implying 13% sequential growth and 61% yoy.

The reason I emphasize growth percentages so much is that I have the strongest conviction that growth is what is sustaining Palantir's valuation. More on this in a moment.

On the bottom line, Q3 adjusted operating margin was 51%, and adjusted gross margin was 84%. Notably, the rule of 40 scored 114%, up 20 points sequentially and 4 points year over year, with the full year 2025 Rule of 40 guided to 102%.

Overall, after looking at the chart above, I agree with the remarks from Alex Karp during the last earnings call:

But a normal enterprise company should not have a Rule of 40 above 100%. A normal enterprise company at our base should not have over 100% U.S. commercial growth should not have 77% growth in the U.S.

For the third consecutive time, management upgraded FY 2025 guidance, now sitting between $4.396 and $4.400 billion. At the midpoint, we're talking about a 53% yoy increase.

I decided to include the following remarks from the CEO that confirm one of my wildest predictions in the US economy (lowering interest rates won't solve unemployment).

And how do we participate in that? Unlike seemingly in the most obvious way, we are downstream from the value creation. So when you see 141% or you see 77% or 63% and you ask, and by the way, with really a workforce that is not growing in any way linearly proportional to that growth and also with a sales force which is declining, which seems improbable.

Palantir sales are ticking up, while its sales force is declining. Naturally, I expect this effect to be accretive to the bottom line of Palantir (and any other company in the US that follows this strategy). In fact, management raised the FCF guidance for FY 2025 to a range of $1.9B - $2.1B, reiterating the earlier expectations of positive GAAP operating income and net income in each quarter of the year.

In regard to what is fueling this growth, I already said that the US segment is significantly more important than the international one. Yet again, the CEO was complaining of "a stagnant Europe". He further added:

And again, you see it like if you look at our numbers, look at how poorly Europe is doing. Look how well America is doing.

Looking at the numbers, Q3 international commercial revenue grew only 10% yoy and 5% sequentially to $152 million. The company is now focusing on targeted growth opportunities in Asia and the Middle East. Based on Karp's earlier comments, I believe Europe is off the table despite "still a significant part" of Palantir's business. I decided to dive a bit more in this regard in the next section.

International Segment Still Under Pressure

We all know what happens with a stock when reputational concerns arise. Just look at the performance of Tesla stock when Elon was leading DOGE earlier this year.

In the last earnings call, there were a few remarks from the CEO that I admit made me sweat.

And then internally, we have, look, we power ICE, we power efforts to defend America and Ukraine and with allies. We're on the front line of all adversaries, including vis-a-vis China. And we support -- we're at ICE and we're -- we've supported Israel.

He further added:

Okay. These are very controversial. I don't know why this is all controversial, but many people find that controversial. Okay? So how do you align people to focus on these things in a way that is actually beneficial for us and our clients.

Based on his words, I believe he is somehow justifying why the company has explosive growth in some regions (i.e., the US), while other geographies are trailing behind. I found it particularly interesting that the company didn't present any slides in the latest investor slide deck in regard to the international segment. Do a "control+f" on "international" and you will find zero results.

This makes me believe that the growth story is still limited to the US. In my last coverage, I was optimistic about the international segment (specifically in the government unit) after a promising partnership in the UK. I said:

the standout is the UK partnership tied to up to £1.5 billion of planned investment and a London defense HQ, which I believe could seed future NATO and European MoD wins.

So far, I haven't seen much traction on this segment. As seen in the chart below, this segment remains a risk to the growth of the company.

Valuation

Speaking of risks, Palantir is trading at 295x next year's earnings. Needless to say, most of the fears of an AI bubble are related to the sky-high premium that investors are willing to pay for this stock.

Look, it is my strongest belief as a market practitioner that valuation begins to matter only when the broader markets are selling off or when the (growth) story around the stock falls apart. That's when funds need to deleverage risk, and more often than not, stocks with high multiples are the ones that end up liquidated first. So far, I am not seeing strong signs to suggest a meaningful selloff in the S&P 500 due to an AI bubble. In fact, I believe we are still far from the pop of the AI bubble. Yes, valuations are high, but the AI story remains intact, despite Burry's short position in Nvidia and Palantir (in the form of put options). Overall, I believe the recent pessimism will be resolved over the next few weeks, and the markets will return to all-time highs. What does this mean for Palantir, considering its high 24-month beta of 2.33?

Well, I wouldn't be surprised to see the stock rerating at higher multiples in the back end of the year. Why? Take a look below at the Street's projections for the top line of the company.

I consider that growth above 30% for the next 5 quarters to justify the sky-high multiples of this stock. Furthermore, take a look at the Street's revisions on the top-line of the company:

All green means the AI bubble is far from bursting.

Conclusion

Despite the underperformance of the stock in the last few weeks, I reiterate my strong buy rating on Palantir.

I see a good chance for the stock to consolidate around the mid-$150s (a key support level) and the high $170s for the next few weeks.

Once the market realizes that the fears of an AI bubble are unwarranted, as the fundamentals haven't shown any signs of deterioration, I believe we could see a rally in the second half of December (especially if Powell cuts the interest rate, which I believe he will).

Looking at Palantir, I see a strong growth story in the US segment, mainly driven by the commercial segment. Most importantly, I see no cracks in this story, especially after management upgraded the guidance for the top line and FCF of FY 2025. I remain bullish on Palantir, and I expect a rally in the back end of the year (likely the second half of December).

data.title: Thoughts From The Muni Desk
data.description:
By Mark Paris, Chief Investment Officer, Head of Municipal Strategies, Invesco Fixed Income | Tim Spitz, Head of Municipal Business Strategy and Development

Tim: Issuers brought new deals to market in record numbers during 2024 and 2025. Do you think that’s likely to continue in 2026?

Mark: Yes, I believe the supply numbers are likely to remain elevated next year. So far this year, new issuance has already surpassed $500 billion, compared to last year’s record tax-exempt issuance of $494 billion.[1] And we still have a month left to go! You’ll remember that many state and local governments held off entering the municipal market for financing until the Federal Reserve finished raising interest rates. They also had plenty of cash on their balance sheets from pandemic-related federal funding. Now that rates are somewhat lower, and federal funding has dried up, we’ve seen issuers return to the market to fund essential infrastructure projects. Inflation has driven up costs, and the deal sizes have grown to compensate. I expect more of the same next year, but the growth rate will likely slow to more typical levels. 2 This moderation should create a healthier technical environment for municipal bonds compared to recent years. We may even see more supportive market technicals next year, with negative net supply from larger levels of reinvestment capital. One forecast, which I believe is aggressive, for 2026 estimates total new issuance at $640 billion, with $470 billion in new money and $170 billion in refundings. This projection also calls for reinvestment proceeds totaling $701 billion, consisting of $520 billion in principal redemptions as well as $181 billion in coupon payments.[2]

Tim: The longest government shutdown in US history has ended after 43 days. The national economy seems to have been resilient, but it will take time to assess the damage, assuming there is any.

Mark: Yes, and municipals seem to have come away from the shutdown relatively unscathed, at least as far as we can tell. At this point we haven’t heard of any long-term repercussions for municipal credits. In fact, the municipal market posted gains over the period, as has happened in four of the previous five government shutdowns.[3] After news broke of a deal brokered in the Senate, the municipal market, like the financial markets overall, reacted positively. Now that the shutdown is over, market participants are looking forward to a heavier, albeit lumpy, macroeconomic data calendar, which should give us a more complete picture of inflation and employment data. Until recently, the only notable releases were the new weekly ADP employment data and unemployment claims.[4] However, we’ll have to be a bit more patient for all of the economic reporting. Even though the federal government has now reopened, it will take time for data releases to start flowing to the markets.

Tim: In the wake of the New York City mayoral election, New York state Governor Kathy Hochul has said publicly that she will not support free bus fares.[5]

Mark: Mayor-Elect Zohran Mamdani’s pledge to voters that he would make buses fast and free when he became mayor may have hit a roadblock. Governor Hochul seems unlikely to go along with his plan, because it would take money away from the Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA), which relies on the farebox for part of its operating costs. Mamdani would need Governor Hochul’s support, as well as the support of the MTA and the New York state legislature, to fulfill his campaign promise.[5] From where I sit, it looks like a non-starter. Let’s remember congestion pricing was implemented in New York City to help the MTA, which is reportedly facing a $452 million shortfall.[5] And the governor has already said no to tax increases.[5] We’ll keep an eye on the developments, but we don’t believe Mamdani’s mayoral term will not have any adverse effects on New York City credits.

About risk

Municipal securities are subject to the risk that legislative or economic conditions could affect an issuer’s ability to make payments of principal and/or interest.

Junk bonds involve greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality.

The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high-quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

All fixed income securities are subject to two types of risk: credit risk and interest rate risk. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the issuer of a security will be unable to make interest payments and/ or repay the principal on its debt. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

Municipal bonds are issued by state and local government agencies to finance public projects and services. They typically pay interest that is tax-free in their state of issuance. Because of their tax benefits, municipal bonds usually offer lower pre-tax yields than similar taxable bonds.

data.title: Rekor Had A Great Quarter, But Financing Risks Remain High Without The ATM
data.description:
Rekor Systems, Inc. (REKR) reported very good 3Q25 results, which had been in part already digested by the market as the company had already pre-reported record revenues and higher margins in October 2025 via press release.

The quarter saw revenue expansion, lacking in 1H25, and significant margin expansion via fixed CoGS and lower OpEx. With such a good quarter, however, the company remains underwater in adjusted EBITDA (-$1.5 million), albeit improving a lot from 1H25.

The question remains whether this will be durable, as indications from the 10-Q point towards non-recurring results. In the meantime, the company continues to lose cash operationally and has exhausted its ATM offering. It will need to tap into debt or equity to sustain activities if the 3Q25 feat cannot be repeated. The financial position of the company was not mentioned during the call.

I maintain a Hold rating on the name because of these risks.

A great quarter

Q3 was a great quarter for Rekor, from most perspectives.

The company expanded revenues by about 35% while reducing CoGS, leading to all of those revenues, and then some more, flowing into gross profitability, which jumped almost 20pp in margin and 100% YoY.

In addition, the company cut on OpEx significantly, both YoY (20%) and QoQ (26%). This fall was shared across all functions, including G&A, selling, R&D, and D&A.

The result was a massive reduction in adjusted EBITDA losses, to -$1.5 million versus -$9.5 million in 3Q24 and -$13 million in 1H25. If adjusted EBITDA is considered a proxy of cash profitability (the company's quarterly interest expenses are relatively low at $0.5 million), then Rekor might be close to reaching pre-tax cash breakeven.

In addition to this, the company secured its largest contract so far, with Georgia's DOT, in October 2025, after the quarter had ended (and therefore not reflected in 3Q25 results). The earnings release for Q3 clarifies that the contract is for $50 million, potentially expandable to $100 million, over 8 years (or about $6.3 million a year, although revenue cadence was not commented on in the release or the call). The company believes it is a sign of wider adoption in the space.

Can it be repeated?

If Q3 is the new normal, then it is a game-changer for Rekor, simply because it would largely dispel the large financing risks (more on this later).

However, I find that evidence of it being recurring is, at least partially, lacking. The most important point comes from the 10-Q for the quarter. Under MD&A, it says that "Revenue increased for the three and nine months ended September 30, 2025, compared to the corresponding periods in 2024, primarily due to higher perpetual license sales in Q3 2025."

The key point is perpetual license sales. Under a perpetual license, the customer pays once and then has the right to keep using the software. It is, therefore, a non-recurring business. The company also separately reports Recurring Revenues on the MD&A, indicating that they jumped a healthy 20%, but only $1 million in dollar terms, versus approximately $3.5 million for the aggregate result.

As seen above, in the past two years, although the company's revenue trend has been positive, revenue has been somewhat lumpy, with significant effects on gross margins, with 10pp moves QoQ being normal.

There were no indications on the call about next quarter's profitability or operations, except for the fact that Q4 will already start to record revenues from the Georgia DOT contract.

Financing remains a key risk

Again, if the company has now reached a new revenue and profitability level, or even more, if it grows from here, financing risks will be reduced for two reasons. First, with smaller adjusted EBITDA losses or even potential profits, the company's financing requirements decrease. Second, with positive EBITDA, it is much more likely that the company could reach some form of lending, which seems not to be available today, given that the company has financed itself primarily via share issuance.

However, if Rekor cannot sustain this level of business, then its financing challenges remain. In Q3, the company lost another $4.8 million in operations ($1.6 million adjusting for working capital requirements) and spent $1.4 million in CAPEX. This gap was covered by issuing $4.7 million in shares.

The problem is that, as reported in the 10-Q under Liquidity and Capital Resources, the company does not have access to an ATM anymore. Therefore, it has not been able to issue equity into the current run taking the price from $1 to $3 and now back to $2 since September 2025.

The lack of an ATM could either indicate confidence in the future (no need for it), or that certain limits have been reached, or that the company is preparing another form of financing (deal equity or debt).

The company should not be affected by the $75 million float restriction on the ATM. However, it has not filed any Form S-1, S-3, or 424B5 after the one authorizing the now extinct ATM.

My fear is that the company could make use of the current high stock price and reach an equity deal at a lower valuation. Or, if it is restricted from issuing because of not having a valid shelf, then it could face liquidity problems. The going concern commentary remains in the 3Q25's MD&A.

In my opinion, Rekor remains too risky. One quarter, which still generated adjusted EBITDA losses, is not enough to consider that the company has initiated a new growth trend. Rekor continues to shed cash, and its financing mechanisms are now more restricted.

For these reasons, and the fact that the company is now almost 2x more expensive than when I last covered it, I maintain a Hold rating on Rekor.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Credit Swoon Sinking Stocks
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Not so long ago (Oct. 10), I wrote about how some credit-sensitive stocks were stumbling. That stumble is now turning into a swoon - and that’s helping sink stocks.

Just look at this week’s MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which is an updated and modified version of the chart I shared in mid-October. It shows the performance of ETFs that track Business Development Companies (BDCs) and the broader financial sector. I also threw in one additional individual stock, Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL). It’s an alternative asset manager and bellwether for the private credit market.

BDC Stocks - No, Things are Not Getting Better

(Data by YCharts)

Things aren’t getting better, clearly. They’re getting worse. The VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) is now down 9.4% on the year. The State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) is roughly flat. As for OWL, what can I say? It’s now off 38% year-to-date, having taken out its spring low recently.

The issue goes back to credit risk. Investors are growing increasingly concerned that private credit firms have been too aggressive lending money the last few years. With the economy slowing and delinquencies rising in select industries, they’re worried more and more proverbial shoes are going to drop.

Then there’s Oracle Corp. (ORCL), the stock I wrote about back on Nov. 10. It was one of the hottest Artificial Intelligence (AI) stocks for a while, but it has done nothing but sell off lately. Meanwhile, in the derivatives market, the cost of insuring Oracle bonds using Credit Default Swaps (CDS) has surged. Translation: Credit worries are percolating in the AI world as well.

We still have seasonality going for us. We still have modestly favorable Federal Reserve policy. But if there’s one thing I’ve learned in my almost three-decade long career analyzing and writing about markets, it’s that you do not want to ignore credit markets. And what I’m seeing there is increasingly troublesome.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Find Smarter Yield With Selective Core Credit
data.description:
Higher yields have returned, but not all credit opportunities are created equal. A selective approach to investment-grade bonds can help investors capture income while maintaining quality.

Advisors are navigating a fixed income market unlike any we've seen in more than a decade. Higher yields, persistent inflation, and tighter financial conditions are creating both opportunities and challenges. With high quality corporate bonds offering yields of approximately 4.9%, and BBB corporates offering 5.1%, investors can build income-generating portfolios without venturing into non-investment grade or illiquid corners of the market. But constructing a resilient core still requires selectivity because not all corporate bonds are created equal.

We believe in a smarter approach to corporate credit. One that provides the benefits of income and potential total return, but with discipline and forward-looking risk control. Through the VanEck Moody’s Analytics IG Corporate Bond ETF (MIG) and the VanEck Moody's Analytics BBB Corporate Bond ETF (MBBB), investors can access a data-driven framework that targets bonds offering attractive value relative to their risk while avoiding issuers more likely to face downgrades or defaults.

Traditional credit ratings, while useful, are often slow to reflect changes in fundamentals. Relying solely on them can expose investors to issuers whose credit health may be deteriorating faster than their ratings imply. In this environment, success depends on not just owning investment-grade credit, but owning the right investment-grade credit.

Deviation from Fair Value Creates Opportunity ‐ Investment-Grade Universe

We use Moody’s Analytics’ proprietary credit risk models to assess the fair value of every bond in the U.S. corporate universe. By comparing each bond’s modeled fair value against its market spread, the process identifies securities offering a significant excess spread above what’s required for their underlying risk. In simple terms, it looks out for bonds that the market is overcompensating relative to their true risk profile.

This systematic approach removes subjective bias and focuses on risk-adjusted value. Portfolios tilt toward issuers with stronger fundamentals and away from those that appear overvalued or at higher risk of downgrade. This means exposure to high-quality, attractively valued bonds designed to participate in the income potential of corporate markets while managing downside risk.

Relative Value Not Driven by Only Rating, Size or Maturity

Selected BBB3, >$1 billion Bonds 5-Year Bonds (As of 9/30/2025)

Despite recent Fed cuts, the broader environment still points to higher structural long-term yields. For advisors, that means an opportunity to build a core income portfolio with better long-term total return potential. By integrating forward-looking credit analytics into portfolio construction, we aim to turn market dispersion into an advantage, one that may enhance risk-adjusted returns over time.

The result has been portfolios that are both resilient in their focus on high-quality issuers and responsive through systematic, model-driven selection that adapts as conditions evolve. As shown below, a selective approach that invests in the most attractively valued bonds increased return by 0.84% versus the broad corporate market with no additional volatility. The BBB segment increased risk slightly but added an additional 1.20% over the broad market.

Selective Approach Drove Improved Returns: 5Y Risk‐Return

Selectivity Adds Value

Below we illustrate how incorporating attractively valued investment-grade credit alongside a traditional core bond exposure can improve portfolio characteristics. Replacing a portion of broad investment-grade holdings with attractively valued bonds increases yield potential with only a modest impact on duration. For investors willing to assume additional credit risk within the investment-grade space, targeted exposure to attractively valued BBB-rated corporates may further enhance total return potential.

Core Bonds is represented by the ICE BofA US Broad Market Index, Attractively Valued IG Corps by the MVIS Moody’s Analytics US Investment Grade Corporate Bond Index and Attractively Valued BBB Corps by the MVIS Moody’s Analytics US BBB Corporate Bond Index. Today’s bond market rewards precision. Broad exposures may deliver yield, but selective exposures can deliver smarter yield. The VanEck Moody’s Analytics IG Corporate Bond ETF and the VanEck Moody’s Analytics BBB Corporate Bond ETF use quantitative credit insight, provided by Moody’s Analytics and used by over 1,000 of the world’s largest banks, asset managers, insurance companies and corporations, to help advisors position client portfolios for today’s evolving fixed income landscape. data.title: Constellation Brands: Fairly Valued With No Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Introduction

Constellation Brands, Inc. (STZ) is one of the biggest global manufacturers and marketers of wine, spirits, and beer, with a wide range of brands under its umbrella. Its stock price had been growing nicely for years, until COVID-19 came and sales logically plummeted. Then a great-looking recovery came in, and STZ showed a massive comeback from $105-110 to $270-275 per share at some point. But unfortunately for those who bought the stock above the $200/share mark, STZ lost almost all of its gains since 2024, and now the 10-year return is negative 5-6%:

The STZ stock was initially bouncing around its 200-day moving average throughout 2022-2024, and then in early January 2025, when they reported for its fiscal Q3 2025 and showed that their Wine & Spirits segment's selling volumes and margins dropped dramatically, the stock dipped by ~17% in a single trading session. And the meltdown hasn't stopped since then, although the key fundamentals (sales and margin dynamics) didn't deteriorate that much to justify the stock's move, at least at first glance.

I'm covering the stock for the first time, so I'll try to be unbiased in my judgment and try to assess the stock without having any position in it.

Assessing Recovery Potential In STZ Stock

As I usually do, let me first take a look at Constellation's most recent financials and operating metrics. They ended their fiscal Q2 2026 on August 31, 2025, and reported for it last month, showing $2.48 billion in sales (-15% YoY) and $3.63 in adjusted EPS (-16% YoY).

While both figures dropped on a YoY basis, their declines were above consensus expectations by 0.95% and 6.51%, respectively, based on Seeking Alpha data. Unfortunately for those who were buying on this double-beating result, the stock kept sliding lower after the Q2:

As far as I understood from their 10-Q and commentary, STZ overshipped beer supplies into Q1/Q2 ahead of summer, and then they had to pull forward a distributor inventory rebalance that normally happens in Q3. In other words, that pull-forward hurt Q2 shipments and the bottom line, so the 6.51% beat on the bottom line we saw for that quarter might have been even bigger. The beat itself came from cost savings (~$65 million in beer cost savings in Q2 alone, $105 million YTD) and solid pricing/mix. On the cost management side, Constellation's approach seems to be working well, and the management team thinks there might be more opportunities ahead for continued operational efficiency gains.

We continue to find ways to make our operations more efficient. A lot of that so far is focused on supplier and sourcing optimization and material and cost innovation. Included in that would be our move to 60-foot railcars and our double stacking within railcars as well as a big initiative around suppliers and terms, if you will. So this is going to continue to be a focus for us over time. We continue to think that there will be opportunities for us in logistics and manufacturing optimization.

Source: STZ's Q2 2026 earnings call

What I don't like is that the management keeps leaning hard on a cyclical explanation for the sales/volumes deterioration, especially stating that macro anxiety among Hispanic consumers, and weak "4,000‑calorie" construction jobs across the US, led to the drop in sales. But I concur with the Morgan Stanley analyst, Dara Mohsenian, who was on the latest earnings call, that the management "doesn't seem to attribute much of the contraction to other more secular factors on the beer category". They see GLP‑1 drugs, cannabis substitution, and lower alcohol consumption among Gen Z as relatively minor factors as of today. I politely disagree with that view - if those were minor factors, we'd not be at the lowest drinking rate in over 90 years now:

The percentage of U.S. adults who say they consume alcohol has fallen to 54%, the lowest by one percentage point in Gallup’s nearly 90-year trend. This coincides with a growing belief among Americans that moderate alcohol consumption is bad for one’s health, now the majority view for the first time.

Source: Gallup's data [August 2025]

Constellation Brands didn't cut its CAPEX guidance, unlike revenue guidance, arguing that "much of FY26 spend is already committed on long‑lead items" and that they need to invest in Mexican beer capacity for the long term. Given the decline in alcohol consumption among youth, I don't see how their sales volumes can rise and translate to the EPS at the past margin. And the margin compression ahead is now a new reality, based on the commentary.

As I think about, again, sticking on margin, the headwinds that we noted last month still remain. We have about 100 basis points of margin headwinds related to fixed costs and incremental tariffs. We have about 60 basis points related to, as Bill just mentioned, keeping our marketing investment in line. Oh, by the way, I misspoke just now. There's 100 basis points with fixed overhead and then there's another 60 points on incremental margins.

They have to spend a lot on marketing to survive the decline in overall usage. Net‑net, even with solid cost‑savings execution that I can't deny, the 2H 2026 margin guide was well below 1H, and it forced Wall Street analysts to revisit the durability of the long‑standing 39-40% beer margin algorithm. We can clearly see that in the latest EPS revisions:

In the Wine & Spirits segment, we saw a structural shift, which resulted in massive drops in shipments because Constellation sold off a big chunk of this business in 2025. Looking at organic, shipments fell by 7%, and the organic sales were down by about 19% YoY in Q2, with the main reasons being 1) softer consumer demand and general macro headwinds in U.S. wholesale wine, and 2) distributor contractual changes and post‑transaction inventory clean‑ups after the divestitures.

Overall, I think that the main problem in STZ's story isn't about the macro issue. The management's confidence that the ongoing deterioration is cyclical and their reluctance to reset long‑term beer margin and growth targets in the face of weaker trends make it hard for investors to reset and move on. That's the problem, and I agree with most skeptics on this front.

The execution risk in this case looks quite substantial to me. Metaphorically speaking, imagine a doctor who misdiagnoses a patient and begins treating the wrong disease. What's the likely outcome? At best, it's further deterioration of the patient's health, let alone the ultimate life risks. The same thing applies to the corporate world. If the management doesn't treat the structural issue seriously - and we see some data points that are quite vocal about it - they're unlikely to revive growth through just CAPEX spending. That's why I wouldn't call STZ a cheap stock, even if it trades at 11.35x and its margins are still relatively high so far.

Assuming no further deterioration in guidance and consensus estimates for Constellation's FY2027 EPS figure of $12.43, and putting a realistic 10-11x earnings multiple on it, I get a price target of ~$130.5/share. That's almost exactly the price that STZ is trading at right now, at the time of this writing.

The Bottom Line

Despite the fact that STZ stock has fallen so deeply since 2024, I think it was fully justified, and I simply see no margin of safety, given that they keep investing a lot of CAPEX into new manufacturing capacities, ignoring the structural risks to their key selling products. If the management admits that the data we have in our hands isn't just about macro issues and temporary demand cuts, but it's a real shift in consumers' behavior, there's a chance that they'll take some measures and try to refocus the business (at least to some extent). So far, I see no upside in STZ, and while it can recover after the massive contraction we saw, the firm's fundamentals don't look bullish to me. I decided to rate STZ as a "Hold"

Thank you for reading!
